sentences.text: My name is Harry Klein-- H-A-R-R-Y, K-L-E-I-N.
I was born March 18, 1922.
I was born in Munkats.
M-U-N-K-A-T-S.
Czechoslovakia.
At that time, it was Czechoslovakia.
We were eight children.
And I was one next to the oldest.
There was five girls, three boys.
And it was a very religious family.
And everybody worked, as far as I remember.
My oldest sister was managing a business.
And the one next to her was a seamstress.
And the younger children-- I don't remember exactly about them.
But they all went to school and-- up to a point.
I, myself, went to Czech school.
And every afternoon, I went to cheder, which was very common in Eastern Europe-- C-H-E-D-E-R.
It was a-- it was a Hebrew school, religious Hebrew school, for a few hours.
Some days I helped in the business-- paperwork, do paperwork.
My father had a-- a-- a wholesale and retail building supply business in Munkats.
And it was his own building, a big building, but a lot of stores rented, and a few apartments rented-- a big yard.
And-- and he sold-- most of the supplies were sold to builders, contractors, like cement, and other items.
It was not a full line of building supply-- a partial line of building supplies.
And my older sister was in the business.
Right, she was in the business.
And I was helping out.

And on Sundays, I remember my father was all dressed up, in Hasidic garb, and went to-- to a place, where he could collect money from his contractors, or made new contracts, sign new contracts for supplies.
What else did he do on Sundays?
Yeah, then the business was closed.
But he was engaged in-- in running-- sometimes they had meetings in hotel lobbies in Munkats-- business meetings.
And that's how the business went on.
My father was working about six, seven hours a day.
And in the evening, then he went home, as far as I remember, to eat dinner.

And after dinner, every night of the week, we went to pray and to learn the Bible, which was very, very common in those years, in most of the Eastern European cities or towns, or even in villages, if they had congregations or synagogues.
Munkacs was a town that consisted of 21,000 Jews-- about 90% Hasidic.
And at least 60% of those Jews were dealers, businesspeople.
Some of them were very, very rich, and they had maids in their homes.
Shuls, congregations-- the town had plenty.
And there were sects, factions, and no real fights.
But people belonged to whatever-- probably handed down to them from their-- their grandparents-- certain shuls.
And they belonged to them.
And most of the people bought their seats outright, when they built it.
They didn't rent it, like here, and pay once a year $200.
It was their seats, with their name on it-- metal plates with the name on it, about 90% of the people.
Those that did not have money, or they didn't own seats, there was tables arranged for them, with benches.
And then the shuls were open-- not like in America.
In some shuls they have guards when they go in.
But the shuls were open to anyone who wanted to pray or to learn.
They belonged to two synagogues.
And one was the synagogue called Belz-- B-E-L-Z-- which was a subsidiary of a Polish big rabbi, or followers of a big rabbi-- a big congregation, possibly 600, 700 families.
And the other congregation was not far from our house.
Actually, it was adjacent to our business building-- also a smaller congregation, maybe 300 families.
And we went there every night.
Because it was close.
We didn't have to walk, after work.
And there was many people there.
And they prayed and learned every night.
OK, that was life in Munkats, as far as I know, till 1938.
The Czechs were very nice toward the Jews.
Like the Swiss or Belgian people, they never bothered any Jews.
And they didn't-- and they cooperated, if a shul needed something, or licenses, or whatever.
They were very, very nice.
There were non-- about 20,000. 50% of the population was non-Jewish.
And then there were some Germans living there, for many years.
They were born there, I assume.
But they spoke German, some of them.
And mostly it was Ukrainians or Hungarians.
And they co-existed very nicely.
We never heard of any pogroms, like in Poland, or Russia-- never heard of.
The police were cooperating with the Jews.
They maintained the order.
And we-- it was a very nice, democratic country.
That was up to 1938.
What I did till the age of 16, or even later, till-- let me see.
I was born in '22-- until '38.
Yeah, to the age of 16-- I was going to school.
Apparently, I was a good student.
I still-- I was excelling in math, and so on.
I was helping out with the bookkeeping, and so on, at the age of 14.
And in the afternoon, I went to learn the Bible.
But, at the age of 16, like so many other Jewish religious people, I started to revolt against the restrictions imposed on me, and my sisters, too-- at least three of them.
And we were not supposed to go to movies.
That's against the Hasidic law.
And so many restrictions, and-- and my father, at least, didn't know.
And I joined a Zionist organization in secret.
And I studied Hebrew the way they speak it in Israel.
I had books.
I don't remember exactly where I studied them.
I couldn't bring them home, that's for sure.
They would have noticed it.
So I was sort of preparing myself for [HEBREW].
[HEBREW] means, in Hebrew, people that go to Israel and build a Jewish homeland.
Anyway, in 1938, apparently the-- Hitler rewarded-- awarded this part of Czechoslovakia to Hungary.
I don't remember the reason.
They cooperated with-- with Nazi Germany.
So from 1938 on, we belonged to Hungary.
Now Hungary had a party, a sort of political party.
It's called Nyilas-- N-Y-I-L-A-S.
And that specialized in bothering and torturing Jews.
That was their main effort, like the KKK here, years ago.
And it was legal.
It was legal.
It was not well spoken of, but Nyilas-- they had their men in the police.
They had their men in the courts.
And so the Jews, from then-- from that day on, were oppressed.
Well, I don't remember official pogroms, like the Russians had, or Poland, where they killed 200 Jews at a time.
But in general, and slowly, they confiscated all Jewish businesses, or they put in Hung-- Hungarian partners, unwillingly.
And I remember clearly when people went home from shul at night, these gangs used to beat them up.
And police stood by.
They turned the other way.
I don't remember, personally, no.
No.
And-- and they-- apparently, the leaders of that party trained in Germany, as far as I can see.
I may be wrong.
But-- and this-- and they had their own men in the police.
And-- and they had a secret service, spying on Jewish people, and so on, and so forth.
So life changed completely.
Some people lost their livelihood within two years.
They took a business away.
I don't remember all the details.
My family-- I-- as far as I remember, we also, in 1940 or '41, we also got a non-Jewish partner, who shared in everything.
Without investment.
That was a prelude to confiscate the whole business, I figure.
He was a contractor that my father did business with, years ago.
Now he became a partner, all of a sudden.
I-- they were assigned by the government, or the local government.
I don't-- these details I don't remember.
But I remember it was bad.
Well, anyway, as far as I remember, we had enough food, at least, through the end-- as far as I remember.
So one year, in 19-- well, I don't remember if I disclosed-- I must have disclosed to my father that I don't see any future staying in Munkats.
So I suggested-- and besides, that was in 1940, already.
We already heard what was going on.
I had an uncle who was a Polish citizen.
This must have been 1939 or 1940.
And they rounded up all Polish Jews.
They were Polish citizens, living in Hungary.
And they transferred-- they transferred them to Poland.
And I never heard of him since.
And he wasn't the only one.
This was a group.
They took them to Poland.
And we never, never heard of him.
So a year after that-- and I probably disclosed to my father, there's no future waiting here for the Germans to come in, or whatever.
Even under the Hungarians, there's no life here.
It's just fear, and so on, and so forth.
So I said-- and he had money.

So I said-- and there were way to get out of Hungary, legally, by purchasing-- by bribing somebody, or getting a passport-- a legal passport to go to a country like Belgium, Holland, Switzerland, which was real democratic, like we had before the Germans.
And he said, no.
Because the Hasidim in general, at that time, said, Palestine doesn't exist.
These are not religious people.
And they're not-- in his opinion, they were not Jewish, sort of.
They're not really Jewish.
Because if they don't observe everything according to the Torah, they're not Jewish.
So he denied my request.
Two years later, I believe, in 1942, the-- the German government, or the local, recruited people my age for forced labor.
These labor groups were sent to the-- to the battlefront, to assist the Germans.
And here is the catch.
Some people my age got different papers-- forged papers, different dates of birth, same location, born in Munkats, a different date-- legal papers.
And they left the town in order to avoid going to a labor camp.
Because the only-- I believe, as far as I-- I was too young.
You had to be a certain age.
So if I had two years younger, they couldn't take me.
Because if they stopped me, how come you're not working, or you're not in Poland-- or wherever-- in Germany?
No, I'm-- here's the papers [INAUDIBLE].
So I was going to another town of [?
Ungvar. ?] And that cost money, too.
Because my father gave me money.
And he also sent money to the family that kept me there, a Jewish family.
Nobody knew.
And I had legal papers.
But the family I lived with-- they knew.
So I stayed there-- not that it helped much.
I wound up in a-- in a ghetto.
And the survival rate of the people that worked in the forced labor camps was 50%.
The survival rate of the Holocaust is about 8% or 10%, at most.
OK, Harry, can I stop you for a minute?
Let's go back a little bit.
How was it that you decided to go to this-- to this family?
How-- your father found out for you?
Well the-- this family had a-- my next door neighbor, they had a store next door to us, this family, in my town.
So they contacted their-- their relatives.
And-- and I think this woman's husband was also in the forced labor camps.
So an extra few dollars helped.
So I paid for my keep.
And in [?
Ungvar-- ?] OK, go ahead.
Any more questions?
In [?
Ungvar-- ?] the name of the town is Ungvar, the same size as Munkats-- not so Hasidic.
I believe the proportion of Jews and non-Jews was about the same.
And I-- I did the work, in a factory, part time, just to ward off the suspicion, I figured.
In 19-- no, I was-- no, more.
I was born in '22.
I was 20.
I was exactly 20.
And I stayed there two years, until-- and-- I-- I managed.
I managed.
I had enough food.
In that family?
Very nice.
They treated me nice.
And they needed the money.
And we had enough food.
And once in a great while, I went home-- for a day, only.
By bus.
I remember.
Yeah.
I had papers.
I had really papers.
I was only afraid in Munkats somebody would notice me.
Because there were people reporting on each other, for money.
They got a few dollars from the government.
And they reported on their fellow Jews, which is not very nice.
But they did it.
So when I got off the bus, I remember, we had a big lake.
And I was walking alongside the lake, instead of the main street.
And I crossed through a little bit.
I stayed home one day.
And I went back.
I may have gotten back at night, the last bus, so nobody-- in [?
Ungvar, ?] I wasn't afraid.
I had papers.
Nobody knew me, exactly.
But in [?
Munkats, ?] I was afraid of fellow Jews, that would recognize me, and report me.
They get a few dollars, a few-- not dollars, but-- and those people were tortured.
If they reported somebody who had forged papers, these people were tortured, and then thrown into labor camps.
So that was the situation, until 1944.
And in 1944, of course, every city-- Elizabeth said a different date.
According to my-- my records, it was in April.
In April, 1944-- probably the beginning of April, the Germans came in.
Germans occupied Hungary.
And since this was a part of Hungary, it was occupied by Germans.
Now their systematic-- and apparently it wasn't the first time that they did this.
Within two weeks-- or first, they-- one week, they didn't do anything.
They got acquainted with the circumstances, or the area, the leaders of the SS, and so on.

But after a week, they posted-- posted notices all over that all the Jewish people from this and this city-- they divided the town by areas, and listed all the streets that have to report here, and here, and here-- this and this date.
And they can carry one suitcase away, and so much, and so much-- all the Jewish people.
That goes for children, women, young men, old men.
And within two weeks, or within seven, eight days, all the Jewish people were in a ghetto.
In the outskirts of the town, there was a big--
--big brick factory.
I ended up in the ghetto, like any other people.
Because I lived in a district.
And when there was a legal notice, with-- with a threat of punishment, I don't know if the punishment was spelled out.
On the bottom was a line, anybody disobeying this order will be punished.
And I don't really remember what the punishment would have been.
But I could imagine.
Because under the Hungarians, we already knew what the punishment would be-- torture and maybe even death.
So as far as I know, nobody dared violating this order.
Yes, I was going with the family, marching.
We didn't have any transportation.
We were marching.
No, first we reported to a big building in the town, occupied by the SS-- or outside.
I don't remember exactly how it was.
But from there, we were marching, with our belongings-- meager belongings-- accompanied by SS soldiers and Hungarian police-- Hungarian policemen.
So-- and we were marching to the outskirts of the town.
And there was the ghetto area-- a big, big, big-- a tremendous area, and a big brick factory.
And-- and they-- everybody-- and as we arrived, everybody occupied an area.
You figured a little warmer.
And then the-- everybody went for straw, to put on the floor a little straw.
And blankets-- as far as I remember, we had with our belongings blankets.
And we occupied a small area.
We were four people, as far as I know-- one, two, three-- two children, one woman and myself.
Yeah, they--
Because husband was somewhere in Poland, working for the Germans, in the labor force.
So she was by herself, with two children and me.
And we occupied an area in the brick factory.
Now as far as I remember, there were areas that had shelter, had a roof over their head.
And some areas were open.
Now I don't really recall what area I was in.
It was-- It was an area and we were sleeping on the floor, on straw.
And we still had some food with us, for about a few days.
And after a few days, they gave us food.
The management, the ghetto management.
I-- as far as I know, the ghetto-- the Germans elected, or appointed, Jewish leaders-- group-- group leaders, and section leaders, and so on.
And that's the system I think they had in Poland, too, or wherever they went in.
Judenrat, it's called in German, Judenrat.
And it was masterminded in Berlin, I figure, so that the Jewish people were governed, sort of, by Jews.
So-- and the Judenrat members cooperated with the Germans.
Otherwise, they-- and they-- they-- they thought they can save themselves.
But in the end, as far as I know, as far as I read, all over, they ended up the same way as the other people.
But while the ghetto was enforced, they had more food, and so on, and so forth.
And these-- so-- and then they formed labor groups, to go to work in the town.
And everybody had to go to work, except for the children, I figure.
And I was working.
But that didn't last too long-- a week or two.
I don't remember, really.
I think it was construction work.
I was strong.
I was young and strong.
It didn't bother me.
And we had enough food at that time.
And-- and we thought this may last, for a year or two, until the war is over.
But we didn't know what-- what's ahead of us.
So one day-- that was only after 10 days, or 12, approximately.
We found out that every day-- heard big loudspeakers.
Every day, around a certain time-- maybe 5:00 PM-- about 800 people, 800 names, will be called to report early in the morning, with their belongings, to a certain place in the ghetto area.
And I wasn't one of the first ones.
And they-- they said it's for resettlement-- official-- official declaration resettlement.
We didn't know what it means-- concentration camps, and so on.
Resettlement-- we already knew it's not-- it's not good.
But we hoped.
Everybody hoped, maybe, by a miracle, he'll survive, somehow.
So every day they-- around 800 people had to report in the morning-- I don't remember exactly-- to a certain area in the ghetto, surrounded by police, and SS guards.
And they read off the names, and lists, and so on.
And after a while, they marched to a railroad station, a railroad track.
And we didn't know-- while we were in the ghetto, we don't know where they were marched.
Because the Germans managed to do everything in secret.
They were masters at it.
So one day, was-- my name was called.
So I took my little belongings.
It was one suitcase, one bag, or something.
And I reported like the others.
And-- and then-- Excuse me, Harry.
How many people were living in the ghetto?
Do you have any idea?
No.
I would say 10,000.
About 10,000 people, or maybe more, not less-- spread out.
It was a tremendous area.
From the time I reported, I left my-- my dwelling in [?
Ungvar-- ?] to the time I reported for resettlement, what they called, it would be-- it would have been around three weeks-- about three weeks.
So the beginning of April-- so I figured, the end of April, we left [?
Ungvar, ?] by railroad.
And about 90 people-- and they were-- they formed groups of 90 in front of every railroad cattle cars-- no windows.
And 90 people had to get in.
They counted them, and recounted them.
And they had to get in.
They gave them a little food.
But the congestion was unbelievable.
So-- and at the end of April, it wasn't so hot yet.
But 90 people in a cattle car, without any-- without any air-- there was one little, you know-- a cattle car has no-- no windows, and the little windows.
And so it was terrible.
